does: This paper derives the information-theoretic limits of language model alignment under KL-divergence constraints, establishing the maximum achievable expected reward improvement for a fixed KL budget. The authors provide a closed-form expression for optimal reward gain that is governed by a Jeffreys divergence term rather than the √KL scaling used in prior work. They reformulate this expression as a covariance under the base model distribution, yielding a practical estimator that predicts alignment gains directly from base model samples without requiring access to the reward function. The analysis covers both the ideal reward setting and extends to the proxy reward case, where misspecification between the true and estimated reward functions affects achievable gains.
connects: This work builds on the long line of research into language model alignment techniques, particularly reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and best-of-N sampling, which have become standard in modern LLM development. It extends prior information-theoretic analyses of reward-KL tradeoffs, improving upon earlier √KL bounds that were used in approximate analyses of alignment algorithms. The paper connects to classical information theory and distribution divergences, specifically invoking Jeffreys divergence to characterize fundamental limits. It also relates to recent work on understanding alignment efficiency, the sample complexity of reward learning, and theoretical guarantees in constrained optimization of language models.
enables: These theoretical characterizations enable better prediction and design of alignment strategies by establishing what gains are fundamentally achievable given computational and KL budgets. The practical estimator developed here could help practitioners assess whether alignment approaches are approaching theoretical limits or leaving room for improvement. The framework opens directions for analyzing the gap between theoretical optima and practical algorithms like PPO or DPO, as well as studying how reward misspecification impacts alignment efficiency at scale. The results may inform resource allocation in alignment efforts and could be extended to multi-objective alignment settings or sequential alignment scenarios.
